fr.title: Gender of Nouns
fr.tip: Every French noun is masculine (le) or feminine (la). Learn the article with each word!

fr.title: Basic -er Verbs
fr.tip: je parle, tu parles, il parle, nous parlons, vous parlez, ils parlent

fr.title: Negation
fr.tip: Wrap the verb with ne...pas: Je ne parle pas français

nl.title: De vs Het
nl.tip: Two articles: 'de' (common) and 'het' (neuter). Most nouns use 'de'!

nl.title: V2 Word Order
nl.tip: Verb is always second: 'Morgen ga ik naar school'

nl.title: Diminutives
nl.tip: Add -je to make things small: huis → huisje, kat → katje

en.title: Articles
en.tip: 'a' before consonant sounds, 'an' before vowel sounds

en.title: Present Simple
en.tip: Add -s for he/she/it: I eat → He eats

de.title: Three Genders
de.tip: der (masculine), die (feminine), das (neuter) — always learn the article WITH the noun!

de.title: du vs Sie
de.tip: du = informal (friends). Sie = formal (strangers). Sie is ALWAYS capitalized!

de.title: Verb Position
de.tip: Main clause: verb ALWAYS second. Heute gehe ich. The verb stays in position 2!

ro.title: Attached Articles
ro.tip: Definite article attaches to the end: casă → casa (the house)

ko.title: SOV Order
ko.tip: Subject-Object-Verb: 나는 사과를 먹어요 (I apple eat)

zh.title: No Conjugation!
zh.tip: Verbs never change! 我吃, 他吃 — same verb

ru.title: Cyrillic Tricks
ru.tip: Some letters look familiar but differ: Р=R, С=S, Н=N

ru.title: No Articles!
ru.tip: No 'the' or 'a' in Russian — context decides!
